name: Barbell Bench Press
instructions: Lie flat on the bench with your eyes under the bar. Grip the bar slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Unrack the bar and hold it directly over your chest with arms fully extended.
Lower the bar in a controlled arc to your mid-chest, keeping elbows at roughly 60 degrees from your torso.
Press the bar back up explosively, fully extending your arms.
Re-rack the bar after completing all reps.
tips: Retract and depress your shoulder blades into the bench to create a stable base.
Keep your feet flat on the floor and drive through them for full-body tension.
A slight natural arch in the lower back is fine — avoid exaggerating it.

name: Incline Dumbbell Press
instructions: Set a bench to 30–45 degrees incline. Sit with a dumbbell on each knee, then kick them up as you lie back.
Hold the dumbbells at shoulder width with palms facing forward, elbows at about 60 degrees from your sides.
Lower the dumbbells slowly until they reach chest level and you feel a stretch across the upper chest.
Press back up in a slight arc, bringing them close together at the top without touching.
Lower with control for each repetition.
tips: Focus on squeezing the upper chest at the top rather than just locking out the arms.
A 30-degree incline targets upper chest more effectively than a steeper angle.

name: Cable Chest Fly
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to chest height. Stand in the center with one handle in each hand.
Step slightly forward into a staggered stance and lean forward a few degrees from the hips.
With a slight bend in your elbows, bring both handles together in front of your chest in a hugging arc.
Squeeze the chest hard at the peak contraction for one second.
Slowly return to the start position, feeling a full stretch across the chest.
tips: Keep the same elbow bend throughout — do not press or extend the arms.
Cables maintain constant tension through the full range of motion.

name: Close-Grip Bench Press
instructions: Lie on a flat bench and grip the barbell with hands shoulder-width apart or slightly narrower.
Unrack the bar and hold it over your lower chest with arms fully extended.
Lower the bar to your lower chest, keeping elbows tucked close to your sides throughout.
Press the bar back up in a straight line, fully extending your arms.
Squeeze the triceps hard at the top of each rep.
tips: Do not go too narrow — shoulder-width is ideal to avoid wrist strain.
Keeping elbows tucked (not flared) shifts emphasis from chest to triceps.

name: Tricep Cable Pushdown
instructions: Attach a bar or rope to a high pulley cable. Stand facing the machine, feet hip-width apart.
Grip the attachment with an overhand grip. Keep your elbows pinned to your sides throughout.
Push the bar down until your arms are fully extended, squeezing the triceps at the bottom.
Slowly allow the weight to return until your forearms are parallel to the floor.
Repeat without letting your elbows drift forward.
tips: A rope attachment allows wrist rotation at the bottom for a greater muscle squeeze.
Lean slightly forward from the hips to help keep elbows locked in position.

name: Overhead Tricep Extension
instructions: Sit on a bench with back support. Hold one dumbbell with both hands, cupping the top plate.
Press the dumbbell overhead so your arms are fully extended.
Keeping your upper arms stationary and elbows pointing straight up, lower the dumbbell behind your head.
Lower until your forearms go past parallel, feeling a deep stretch in the long head of the tricep.
Press back to full extension by contracting the triceps.
tips: The overhead position maximally stretches the long head of the tricep — the largest portion.
Keep your core braced and avoid arching your lower back as you press overhead.

name: Barbell Bent-Over Row
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart. Hinge at the hips until your torso is at about 45 degrees, keeping a neutral spine.
Grip the bar just outside shoulder-width with an overhand grip. Let it hang at arm's length.
Drive your elbows back and up, pulling the bar into your lower ribcage.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together hard at the top for one second.
Lower the bar with control back to the starting position.
tips: Maintain a braced core and neutral lower back throughout — rounding under load risks injury.
Pull with your elbows, not your hands, to better engage the lats over the biceps.

name: Lat Pulldown
instructions: Sit at a lat pulldown machine and secure your thighs under the pads. Grip the bar wider than shoulder-width.
Lean back slightly and depress your shoulder blades before initiating the pull.
Pull the bar down toward your upper chest, leading with your elbows and driving them toward your hips.
Squeeze the lats at the bottom when the bar reaches your chest.
Slowly extend your arms back overhead, achieving a full stretch before the next rep.
tips: Avoid using momentum or shrugging the shoulders — keep it controlled.
A slight lean back (15–20 degrees) allows for a better range of motion.

name: Single-Arm Dumbbell Row
instructions: Place one hand and the same-side knee on a flat bench for support. Hold a dumbbell in the opposite hand.
Let the dumbbell hang at arm's length directly below your shoulder, with a neutral spine.
Row the dumbbell up toward your hip, driving your elbow back and keeping it close to your body.
At the top, squeeze the lat and upper back for a full contraction.
Lower slowly to full arm extension and repeat.
tips: Allow your shoulder blade to protract at the bottom and retract fully at the top for maximum range.
You can go heavier here than many back exercises — use a challenging weight without compromising form.

name: Seated Cable Row
instructions: Sit at the cable row station and place your feet on the platform with a slight bend in the knees.
Grip the close-grip handle with a neutral (palms facing each other) grip.
Sit upright with a neutral spine. Pull the handle to your lower ribcage by driving your elbows back.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at full contraction and hold for one second.
Return the handle forward with control, allowing your shoulder blades to fully protract.
tips: Avoid rocking your torso backward — this uses momentum rather than muscle.
Think about pulling your elbows through the wall behind you to engage the mid-back.

name: Barbell Curl
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, holding a barbell with an underhand grip at shoulder width.
Keep your elbows pinned at your sides and your upper arms stationary throughout.
Curl the barbell up by contracting the biceps, bringing the bar to shoulder level.
Squeeze the biceps hard at the top, then lower with control through the full range of motion.
Do not let momentum swing the bar — keep the movement strict.
tips: A barbell allows you to handle more weight than dumbbells, making it ideal for progressive overload.
An EZ-bar can substitute to reduce wrist discomfort while still targeting the biceps.

name: Hammer Curl
instructions: Stand or sit holding a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip (palms facing your torso).
Keep elbows close to your sides and upper arms stationary.
Curl both dumbbells up simultaneously, keeping the neutral grip throughout.
Bring the dumbbells to shoulder height and squeeze at the top.
Lower with control back to full extension.
tips: The neutral grip shifts emphasis to the brachialis, adding thickness to the arm.
Alternate arms if you prefer — alternating allows a slight rotation for a longer range.

name: Barbell Back Squat
instructions: Set the barbell on a squat rack at upper-chest height. Position the bar across your upper traps.
Grip the bar slightly wider than shoulder-width, step back, and stand with feet shoulder-width apart, toes slightly turned out.
Brace your core, take a deep breath, and descend by breaking at the hips and knees simultaneously.
Squat until your thighs are at least parallel to the floor, keeping your chest up and knees tracking over your toes.
Drive through the entire foot to stand back up, extending hips and knees together.
tips: Depth is critical — squatting to parallel or below ensures proper glute and hamstring engagement.
Keep your weight balanced across the whole foot, not just the heels or toes.
Record yourself from the side to check form at the bottom.

name: Romanian Deadlift
instructions: Stand holding a barbell at hip level with an overhand grip, feet hip-width apart.
Keeping a slight bend in your knees and your back flat, hinge forward at the hips by pushing them back.
Lower the bar along your legs until you feel a significant stretch in your hamstrings.
Drive your hips forward to return to standing, squeezing your glutes at the top.
Do not allow your lower back to round at any point.
tips: Think of it as a hip hinge, not a squat — your knees should barely move.
The bar should stay very close to your legs throughout the entire movement.

name: Barbell Hip Thrust
instructions: Sit on the floor with your upper back against a flat bench. Roll a barbell over your hips (use a pad for comfort).
Plant your feet flat on the floor, about hip-width apart with knees bent at roughly 90 degrees.
Drive through your heels and upper back to push your hips toward the ceiling.
At the top, your torso should be parallel to the floor with a strong glute squeeze. Hold for one second.
Lower your hips back down under control without letting them touch the floor between reps.
tips: The hip thrust produces the highest glute activation of any exercise.
Tuck your chin slightly and keep your ribs down to avoid hyperextending the lower back at the top.

name: Leg Press
instructions: Sit in the leg press machine with your lower back flat against the pad. Place feet hip-width apart in the middle of the platform.
Release the safety handles and lower the platform by bending your knees toward your chest.
Descend until your knees form a 90-degree angle or your thighs reach your chest.
Press the platform back up by extending your knees and hips, without fully locking out.
Control the descent on every rep.
tips: Placing your feet higher on the platform shifts more load to the glutes and hamstrings.
Never allow your lower back to peel off the pad at the bottom — reduce range of motion if this occurs.

name: Dumbbell Walking Lunge
instructions: Stand upright holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides.
Step forward with one foot and lower your body until your front thigh is parallel to the floor.
Your back knee should hover just above the ground. Keep your torso upright.
Push off the front foot to bring the back foot forward, stepping into the next lunge.
Continue alternating legs for the prescribed number of steps per leg.
tips: Take a long enough stride so your front knee does not drift past your toes.
For more glute emphasis take longer strides; for more quad emphasis take shorter strides.

name: Seated Leg Curl
instructions: Sit in the leg curl machine and adjust the pad so it rests just above your heels.
Grip the handles and keep your back flat against the seat.
Curl your heels down and back toward the seat by contracting your hamstrings.
Squeeze the hamstrings hard at the peak contraction.
Slowly extend back to the starting position under full control.
tips: The seated position places the hamstrings under tension at a longer length, advantageous for hypertrophy.
Avoid letting the weight stack touch between reps to keep constant tension.

name: Barbell Overhead Press
instructions: Set a barbell in a rack at upper-chest height. Grip the bar just outside shoulder-width with an overhand grip.
Unrack the bar and hold it at shoulder level with your elbows slightly in front of the bar.
Brace your core and press the bar straight overhead until your arms are fully locked out.
As the bar passes your face, move your head slightly back, then re-engage under the bar at the top.
Lower the bar under control back to the shoulders and repeat.
tips: Squeeze your glutes and brace your core to prevent lower back hyperextension.
The bar should travel in a nearly straight vertical line.

name: Dumbbell Lateral Raise
instructions: Stand with a dumbbell in each hand at your sides, with a slight bend in your elbows.
Raise both arms out to the sides simultaneously, leading with your elbows, until your arms are parallel to the floor.
At the top, your pinkies should be slightly higher than your thumbs.
Hold for a brief squeeze at the top, then lower slowly and with control.
Do not allow momentum or body swing — keep the movement strict.
tips: Lateral raises are essential for shoulder width — they cannot be replaced by pressing movements.
Go lighter than you think — strict form for high reps is far more effective than heavy with momentum.

name: Cable Face Pull
instructions: Set a rope attachment on a high pulley. Stand facing the machine and grip the rope ends with both hands.
Step back a few feet so there is tension on the cable. Stand with feet shoulder-width apart.
Pull the rope toward your face, separating the rope ends as you pull so they come to either side of your head.
Focus on driving your elbows back and squeezing your rear delts and upper traps at full contraction.
Slowly return the rope to the starting position with control.
tips: Face pulls target the rear delts and external rotators, which are critical for shoulder health.
Aim for higher rep ranges (15–20) with lighter weight to focus on the mind-muscle connection.

name: EZ-Bar Curl
instructions: Stand holding an EZ-bar with a shoulder-width underhand grip on the angled inner grips.
Keep your elbows pinned to your sides and stand upright with a neutral spine.
Curl the bar up toward your shoulders by contracting your biceps.
Squeeze the biceps hard at the top, with the bar at shoulder height.
Lower the bar with control back to full arm extension.
tips: The angled grips reduce wrist and elbow stress compared to a straight barbell.
Control the eccentric (lowering) phase — a 2–3 second lower increases time under tension.

name: Skull Crusher
instructions: Lie on a flat bench holding an EZ-bar with an overhand grip, arms extended directly above your chest.
Keeping your upper arms perpendicular to the floor and stationary, bend your elbows to lower the bar toward your forehead.
Stop just before the bar reaches your forehead and feel the stretch in the triceps.
Extend your elbows to press the bar back to the starting position.
Keep your elbows from flaring outward throughout.
tips: An EZ-bar is preferred over a straight bar to reduce wrist stress.
Lowering slightly behind your head instead of to the forehead increases the long head stretch.
